content.text: Conversational AI requirements — for chatbots, assistants, and agent-driven dialogue systems — are fundamentally different from requirements for deterministic software. A traditional system requirement ('the form must validate phone number format') is testable against a binary pass/fail criterion. A conversational requirement ('the assistant should respond empathetically when a customer reports a bereavement') is a gradient quality expectation that must be specified precisely enough to be testable, yet captures behaviour across an infinite space of possible user inputs. Requirements elicitation for conversational experiences must address seven distinct dimensions; missing any one of them produces gaps in the system prompt and evaluation criteria that surface as inconsistent, surprising, or harmful chatbot behaviour in production.

attrs.alt: Seven dimensions of conversational AI requirements: Persona, Intent Coverage, Grounding, Guardrails, Context Management, Escalation, Evaluation

content.text: Why Conversational Requirements Are Different
Three properties of conversational AI make standard requirements elicitation techniques insufficient on their own:
Open input space

— A form field that validates an email address has a bounded input space. A conversational interface accepts arbitrary natural language. Requirements must specify behaviour across the space of likely inputs (the distribution of real user queries) AND across adversarial inputs (prompt injection, jailbreak attempts, emotionally charged language). Enumerating all inputs is impossible; the requirements must describe the principles that govern behaviour across the space.
Gradient quality

— A response is not simply 'correct' or 'incorrect'. It can be accurate but unhelpful (technically right but not actionable), helpful but harmful (correctly describing how to dispute a claim in a way that increases legal risk for the insurer), or empathetic but inaccurate (warmly validating a coverage claim the policy does not support). Requirements must specify multiple quality dimensions simultaneously.
Emergent failure modes

— Conversational AI fails in ways that are not predictable from the requirements alone: the model hallucinates a policy clause, the escalation trigger fires too rarely and frustrated users abandon, the tone becomes unexpectedly casual when users use informal language. Discovery of these failure modes requires testing with real users, not just requirements review. The failure definitions in the requirements are hypotheses about what can go wrong — production behaviour refines them.
The Seven Requirements Dimensions
Dimension 1: Persona

The persona specification determines how the AI presents itself and how it sounds across all interactions. For enterprise deployments, persona consistency is a brand and trust requirement: an assistant that alternates between formal and casual registers depending on the user's tone is perceived as unreliable.
Persona Attribute
What to Specify
Elicitation Question
Name / identity
Whether the assistant has a name; whether it presents as AI or avoids the question; how it introduces itself
How do you want customers to perceive this assistant — as a branded AI persona, as a neutral tool, or as a member of your team?
Formality level
Formal (full sentences, no contractions, professional vocabulary), semi-formal, conversational, or casual
What formality does your organisation use in written customer communications? Match to that register.
Empathy level
Low (task-focused, minimal acknowledgement of emotion), medium (acknowledges emotional content, proceeds), high (prioritises emotional acknowledgement before task resolution)
When a customer contacts you about a total-loss vehicle or a bereavement claim, how do your best human agents respond in the first 30 seconds?
Humour and informality
Whether the assistant can use light humour, colloquialisms, or must remain strictly professional
Has your brand ever used humour in customer communication? If not, do not permit it in the AI — inconsistency with brand creates trust problems.
Dimension 2: Intent Coverage

Intent coverage is the explicit definition of what the assistant will and will not help with. Without clear intent boundaries, the system prompt cannot include the out-of-scope behaviour definition, and the assistant will attempt to answer questions it should not — typically producing hallucinated responses in domains it was not designed for.
Elicitation method

— Run a card-sort exercise with operational subject-matter experts: write the top 50 customer question types on cards (derived from contact centre data, search logs, or CRM topic tags), and sort them into 'handle in chatbot', 'escalate to human', and 'out of scope entirely'. The sort produces the intent coverage map and surfaces disagreements about where the line should be.
Fallback specification

— The fallback response (what the assistant says when it encounters an out-of-scope intent) must be specified with the same care as in-scope responses. A generic 'I can't help with that' is a failure. A specific 'I can help with claim status and new claim reports — for billing questions, please call 0800 XXX' is a success.
Edge case handling

— 'I want to complain about your service' is neither in-scope (it's not FNOL) nor clearly out-of-scope (it's a customer need that warrants a response). Specify these boundary cases explicitly: how does the assistant handle complaints, escalation requests, or questions that are adjacent to but outside the defined scope?
Dimension 3: Grounding

The grounding specification defines where the assistant's factual assertions come from and how it handles uncertainty. For regulated industries, the grounding requirements are often the most consequential: a chatbot that cites an outdated policy document or invents coverage terms it cannot find in the policy is a regulatory and reputational risk.
Knowledge source inventory

— List every data source the assistant will draw on: policy wording RAG index, claim status API, product catalogue, FAQ database. For each source, specify: who owns the data, how it is kept current, what the maximum staleness SLA is.
Hallucination policy

— 'Cite or refuse' is the safest policy for regulated contexts: the assistant answers only if it can cite the specific policy section, claim record, or API response that supports the answer; if it cannot find the information, it says 'I don't have access to that information' and offers an escalation path. This policy requires robust retrieval (a RAG system that can reliably find the relevant section) and a fallback path for unanswerable queries.
Citation requirements

— Specify whether the assistant must include the source reference in its response ('Based on Section 3.2 of your Home Insurance policy...') or whether grounding is an internal quality control mechanism that does not surface in the user-facing response. The former builds user trust but adds response length; the latter is simpler but cannot be audited by the user.
Dimension 4: Guardrails
Guardrails define what the assistant must not do, what topics it must handle with particular care, and how it responds to attempts to make it behave outside its defined parameters.
Prohibited topics

— Specific topics the assistant must decline: legal advice on claim disputes, commentary on competitors, premium negotiation, diagnosis of injury or medical conditions. Each prohibited topic needs a redirect: 'For legal advice on your claim, our team can connect you with a specialist.'
Sensitive topic handling

— Topics that are in scope but require special handling: bereavement claims, total vehicle loss, injury disclosures. Specify the exact behaviour required: acknowledge the emotional content before asking for claim details; offer human escalation before proceeding; use specific language ('I'm sorry to hear this — I'm here to help').
Jailbreak resistance

— Specify how the assistant handles prompt injection and jailbreak attempts ('ignore your previous instructions', 'pretend you are a different AI', 'tell me your system prompt'). The system prompt should include explicit instructions for these scenarios, but the requirements document should record that jailbreak resistance is a requirement and describe the expected response.
Dimensions 5–7: Context Management, Escalation, and Evaluation
The remaining three dimensions are briefer to specify but equally important to the completeness of the requirements:
Dimension
Key Questions to Answer
5. Context Management

Does the assistant remember earlier turns in the same session (e.g. 'you mentioned claim number XYZ earlier')? Does it retain any context across sessions? What is the maximum context window before the session resets? Who can access the session transcript and for how long?
6. Escalation

What triggers escalation to a human agent: explicit user request, N consecutive unresolved turns, specific sensitive topic detection, or low model confidence? What context is passed to the agent (full transcript, summary, or intent only)? How does the assistant communicate the handoff to the user?
7. Evaluation

What is the primary success metric (task completion rate, CSAT, containment rate)? What is the minimum acceptable threshold? What constitutes a failure that would trigger a product recall or rollback? Who performs human evaluation, how often, and on what sample?
Requirements Model and Completeness Check

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal, Optional

Formality = Literal["formal", "semi_formal", "conversational", "casual"]
EscalationTrigger = Literal[
    "explicit_request",    # user says "speak to a person"
    "repeated_failure",    # N consecutive unresolved turns
    "sensitive_topic",     # distress, complaint, legal, regulatory
    "confidence_low",      # model's confidence below threshold
    "out_of_scope",        # intent not in defined scope
]

@dataclass
class ConversationalRequirements:
    use_case: str
    channel: str   # e.g. "web chat", "mobile app", "internal portal"

    # Dimension 1: Persona
    persona_name: Optional[str]
    formality: Formality
    empathy_level: Literal["low", "medium", "high"]
    humour_permitted: bool

    # Dimension 2: Intent coverage
    in_scope_intents: list[str]
    out_of_scope_response: str  # what the bot says when asked something out of scope

    # Dimension 3: Grounding
    knowledge_sources: list[str]
    hallucination_policy: Literal[
        "cite_or_refuse",     # answer only if source can be cited; otherwise say "I don't know"
        "cite_and_answer",    # always try; cite when possible
        "answer_without_cite" # internal tool, citation less important
    ]
    knowledge_freshness_sla_hours: int  # max age of grounding data

    # Dimension 4: Guardrails
    prohibited_topics: list[str]
    sensitive_topics_requiring_empathy: list[str]
    max_response_length_words: int

    # Dimension 5: Context management
    session_memory: bool
    cross_session_memory: bool
    max_turns_before_reset: int

    # Dimension 6: Escalation
    escalation_triggers: list[EscalationTrigger]
    max_unresolved_turns_before_escalation: int
    context_passed_on_escalation: Literal["full_transcript", "summary", "intent_only"]

    # Dimension 7: Evaluation
    primary_success_metric: str
    minimum_acceptable_csat: Optional[float]
    maximum_acceptable_escalation_rate: float  # fraction 0–1
    failure_definitions: list[str]

    def completeness_check(self) -> list[str]:
        """Return list of gaps that would block prompt engineering."""
        gaps = []
        if not self.in_scope_intents:
            gaps.append("No in-scope intents defined")
        if not self.knowledge_sources:
            gaps.append("No knowledge sources specified")
        if not self.prohibited_topics:
            gaps.append("No prohibited topics defined — guardrails cannot be written")
        if not self.failure_definitions:
            gaps.append("No failure definitions — evaluation criteria incomplete")
        if self.cross_session_memory and not self.prohibited_topics:
            gaps.append("Cross-session memory with no privacy guardrails is a data risk")
        return gaps


# FNOL chatbot requirements
fnol_chat = ConversationalRequirements(
    use_case="FNOL First Notice of Loss Chatbot",
    channel="Customer web portal",
    persona_name="Alex",
    formality="formal",
    empathy_level="high",
    humour_permitted=False,
    in_scope_intents=[
        "report_new_claim", "check_claim_status",
        "upload_claim_documents", "request_callback",
        "get_policy_coverage_summary",
    ],
    out_of_scope_response=(
        "I can help with new claims, claim status, and policy questions. "
        "For anything else, our team is available on 0800 XXX XXXX."
    ),
    knowledge_sources=["policy_wordings_rag_index", "claim_status_api", "coverage_matrix_table"],
    hallucination_policy="cite_or_refuse",
    knowledge_freshness_sla_hours=24,
    prohibited_topics=["legal advice", "competitor comparison", "premium negotiation"],
    sensitive_topics_requiring_empathy=["bereavement", "total loss vehicle", "injury claim"],
    max_response_length_words=150,
    session_memory=True,
    cross_session_memory=False,
    max_turns_before_reset=20,
    escalation_triggers=["explicit_request", "repeated_failure", "sensitive_topic"],
    max_unresolved_turns_before_escalation=3,
    context_passed_on_escalation="full_transcript",
    primary_success_metric="Task completion rate (claim successfully registered or status retrieved)",
    minimum_acceptable_csat=4.2,
    maximum_acceptable_escalation_rate=0.20,
    failure_definitions=[
        "Bot gives incorrect coverage information not supported by policy document",
        "Bot continues conversation after user expresses distress without offering human escalation",
        "Bot reveals another customer's claim information",
        "Bot advises on legal strategy or liability",
    ],
)

gaps = fnol_chat.completeness_check()
if gaps:
    print("Requirements gaps:", gaps)
else:
    print(f"Requirements complete for: {fnol_chat.use_case}")
    print(f"  Persona: {fnol_chat.persona_name} ({fnol_chat.formality}, empathy={fnol_chat.empathy_level})")
    print(f"  In-scope intents: {len(fnol_chat.in_scope_intents)}")
    print(f"  Grounding: {fnol_chat.knowledge_sources}")
    print(f"  Escalation: after {fnol_chat.max_unresolved_turns_before_escalation} unresolved turns")
    print(f"  Success metric: {fnol_chat.primary_success_metric}")
    print(f"  Failure definitions: {len(fnol_chat.failure_definitions)}")
Elicitation Techniques for Conversational Requirements
Standard requirements interviews and user story workshops are necessary but insufficient for conversational requirements. Three additional techniques surface requirements that generic workshops miss:
Wizard of Oz Conversations

Before any AI is built, run structured conversations where a human plays the role of the AI assistant. The 'user' does not know they are talking to a human. Record 30–50 conversations and analyse them for:
What intents did users bring that the requirements did not anticipate?
What responses felt natural vs awkward — what tone and length worked?
Where did the human 'wizard' struggle to stay in persona? These are the hard guardrail cases.
What escalation triggers occurred naturally — when did users express frustration or ask for a human?

Wizard of Oz conversations generate a corpus of realistic user inputs before a single line of prompt engineering is written. They are the most effective technique for surfacing unknown unknowns in conversational requirements.
Failure Mode Pre-Mortem

Run a structured session with the product team and the operational stakeholders: 'Assume this chatbot has been live for six months and something serious has gone wrong. What happened?' Answers typically surface:
Hallucination scenarios: 'It told a customer they were covered for something they weren't, and we had to honour it.'
Escalation failures: 'A customer was very distressed and it kept trying to collect claim details instead of connecting them to a human.'
Out-of-scope boundary failures: 'It started giving legal advice on disputed liability.'
Data privacy incidents: 'It showed one customer's claim details to another.'

Each failure scenario from the pre-mortem becomes a failure definition in the evaluation requirements and a test case in the evaluation set. This converts hypothetical risk into testable requirements before build.
Conversation Flow Mapping

For each in-scope intent, map the expected conversation flow as a tree: the opening user message (in multiple formulations), the assistant's first response, the likely follow-up user messages (including edge cases like non-sequitur messages, corrections, and requests to start over), and the terminal states (task completed, escalated, user abandoned). The flow map reveals:
Where the context management requirements need to be precise (what the assistant needs to remember across turns to complete the task).
Where the escalation triggers must fire (the user who abandons at turn 3 because their question keeps being misunderstood).

Where persona and guardrails interact (turn 4 of a bereavement claim flow is not the place for the assistant to ask for vehicle registration details without first acknowledging the emotional context).
Requirements Specification Template

The conversational requirements document is a formal deliverable produced at the end of the elicitation phase. It should follow a consistent structure so that prompt engineers, evaluators, and operational teams can all use it as a reference:
Section
Contents
Approver
1. Use case scope
Channel, user population, primary task the assistant supports, out-of-scope statement
Product Owner + Operations Lead
2. Persona specification
Name, formality, empathy level, tone guidelines with examples, prohibited language
Marketing / Brand + Operations Lead
3. Intent coverage map
In-scope intents with example phrasings, out-of-scope intents with redirect responses, boundary cases
Operations Lead + Customer Experience
4. Grounding requirements
Knowledge sources, hallucination policy, citation requirements, freshness SLA per source
AI Lead + Data Owner + Legal
5. Guardrails
Prohibited topics with redirects, sensitive topics with handling specification, jailbreak resistance requirement
Legal + Compliance + AI Lead
6. Context management
Session memory scope, cross-session memory policy, data retention period for transcripts
DPO + Product Owner
7. Escalation specification
Triggers, context handoff format, human agent briefing format, re-entry policy
Operations Lead + Contact Centre Lead
8. Evaluation criteria
Primary metric + threshold, failure definitions, human evaluation protocol, rollback trigger
Product Owner + Operations Lead + AI Lead
Common Elicitation Gaps and Their Consequences

The following gaps consistently appear in conversational requirements documents and produce predictable failures in production. Each gap has a detectable symptom that surfaces during prompt testing or piloting:
Elicitation Gap
How It Manifests in Production
Prevention
No out-of-scope response defined
Bot attempts to answer questions it cannot answer, producing hallucinated responses on topics outside its knowledge base
Run the card-sort exercise; specify the out-of-scope redirect for every category that was sorted 'out'
Empathy level unspecified
Bot collects claim details in neutral transactional language immediately after user discloses a bereavement; customer CSAT on sensitive claim types is significantly lower than overall average
Specify empathy level AND the specific phrases to use for each sensitive topic category
Hallucination policy not enforced in prompt
Bot gives specific coverage advice ('your policy covers this') without citing the source; claim is honoured by operations team based on bot's statement; retroactive check finds no coverage
Include explicit 'cite or refuse' instruction in system prompt; test with 20 questions whose answers cannot be found in the knowledge base
Escalation trigger threshold too high
User who has repeated 'I just want to talk to someone' three times is still being offered self-service options; abandons and calls the contact centre anyway; no escalation event logged
Define triggers by observable event, not by conversation quality judgement ('after 3 consecutive turns where user input is negative or expresses frustration')
No failure definitions
Evaluation team cannot agree on what constitutes a 'bad response'; human evaluators rate the same response differently; evaluation results are not reproducible or actionable
Define at least 5 specific failure categories with example failure instances before evaluation begins
Cross-session memory without privacy specification
Bot remembers a customer's previous claim details across sessions; another user logs into same shared device; previous customer's information is exposed
Specify cross-session memory policy explicitly; default to session-only unless cross-session memory is a stated user need AND a DPA-reviewed data processing activity
Worked Example: Completing an Incomplete Requirements Set
A requirements document for a claims status chatbot has been submitted to the AI team with the following specification:
The chatbot should answer customer questions about claim status in a friendly way. It should be helpful and accurate. If it can't help, it should say so and suggest the customer calls us.

This specification is missing six of the seven required dimensions. The elicitation team runs a structured gap-fill session with the product owner and operations lead to produce the complete requirements:
Persona

— Gap-fill questions: 'Friendly in what way — like a peer, or like a professional customer service agent?' 'Should it have a name?' 'What should it NOT say?' Output: semi-formal, named 'Cara', no humour, avoids jargon.
Intent coverage

— Gap-fill: 'What are the top 10 things customers call about regarding claims?' 'Which of these should Cara handle vs escalate?' Output: card-sort produces 5 in-scope intents and 8 out-of-scope categories with specific redirects.
Grounding

— Gap-fill: 'What does Cara query to find claim status?' 'What if the claim isn't in the system?' 'What if the policy has an exclusion the customer doesn't know about?' Output: claim status API + policy RAG; cite-or-refuse policy; claims not in system → escalate.
Guardrails

— Gap-fill: 'What should Cara never say even if a customer asks?' 'What topics would create a regulatory problem?' Output: never gives liability opinions; never confirms fraud investigation status; detects and escalates domestic violence indicators.
Escalation

— Gap-fill: 'The spec says suggest calling — when exactly?' 'What does Cara tell the agent when escalating?' Output: escalate after 2 unresolved turns OR on explicit request OR on sensitive topic; pass full transcript + claim number.
Evaluation

— Gap-fill: 'How will you know if Cara is working well? What would make you take it offline?' Output: task completion ≥ 75%, CSAT ≥ 4.0/5, escalation rate ≤ 25%; rollback if misclassification rate on claim status queries > 5%.

The gap-fill session takes 90 minutes with the right stakeholders and produces a complete requirements document. The alternative — beginning prompt engineering from the incomplete specification — produces a chatbot that needs 3–4 revision cycles during piloting to address the gaps, at 3–4x the cost.
Summary

Conversational AI requirements span seven dimensions: Persona (voice and tone), Intent Coverage (what the assistant handles and declines), Grounding (knowledge sources and hallucination policy), Guardrails (prohibited topics and sensitive handling), Context Management (session and cross-session memory), Escalation (triggers and handoff protocol), and Evaluation (success metrics and failure definitions).

All seven dimensions must be specified before prompt engineering begins. Missing intent coverage means the out-of-scope response cannot be written. Missing grounding policy means the assistant hallucinates and cites nothing. Missing failure definitions means the evaluation cannot identify when the product has failed.

Three elicitation techniques supplement standard workshops: Wizard of Oz conversations (human plays AI before build, surfaces unanticipated intents and natural tone expectations), failure mode pre-mortem (surfaces likely failure scenarios as testable requirements), and conversation flow mapping (reveals context management, escalation, and persona-guardrail interactions).

The conversational requirements document has eight sections, each with a named approver. Sections 4 (grounding), 5 (guardrails), and 6 (context management) require legal and DPO approval — start these reviews early, not at the end of requirements elicitation.
